text: The patient is positioned in the right lateral position, and the surgeon utilizes both robotic arms along with an extra assist pro-car for the assistant during the procedure.
The surgeon uses robotic arms to lift the spleen, retracting the hilum. The peritoneal attachments are carefully dissected layer by layer to expose the splenic artery.
The surgeon carefully navigates around the tip of the pancreas, which is typically located near the splenic hilum, ensuring precise manipulation to avoid damage.
The surgeon uses fluorescent technology, injected by the anesthesiologist, to visualize and precisely dissect and isolate the vessels, enhancing visibility despite the challenging anatomy.
The surgeon clearly identifies the Hispanic vessels and safely dissects around them, isolating the vessels from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon clips and cuts the vessels individually after isolating them, using robotic arms for precision.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the gastroesophageal ligaments, which contain the short gastric vessels, ensuring protection of the stomach during the dissection.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to detach all lateral peritoneal attachments of the spleen, including the colon and ligaments, achieving a quick and bloodless separation.
The surgeon irrigates the splenic bed to confirm hemostasis after the spleen is completely resected. A T-cell bio-glue and hemostatic agent is applied to enhance hemostasis in the area.
The surgeon inserts the resected spleen into a large retrieval bag, preparing it for removal from the surgical field.
